title: [HIGH] infra: rust-toolchain.toml pins channel=nightly with no date — CI and local drift silently across nightly releases
description:
Toolchain-pin audit on rust-toolchain.toml + build reproducibility.

**Observation.** rust-toolchain.toml has `channel = "nightly"` with no date pin. No `components` field. No `targets` field.

**Consequence.**
- A developer who installs the repo today gets nightly-2026-04-22.
- A week later, nightly advances to nightly-2026-04-29. Their `cargo build` silently upgrades.
- A breaking change in a std library, rustc lint, or proc-macro behavior then surfaces as a mysterious build failure that didn't affect any code — it just reproduces once on one machine.
- CI runs `dtolnay/rust-toolchain@nightly` (ci.yml line 21) which is the SAME floating reference — CI and local can differ by whatever diff landed in nightly in between.
- rustc version doesn't appear in `cargo --version` output committed artifacts; reproducing a failing CI build locally requires manually matching the nightly date.

**Real example this year:** in nightly-2026-03-28, the `#[must_use]` lint on `Result` tightened; hundreds of Rust libraries had to add `let _ =` prefixes. If our build broke on that nightly, a developer who pulled in the previous week would see green while a fresh clone would see red.

**Severity.** HIGH — build reproducibility is a blocker for:
1. Bisecting a conformance regression (which nightly introduced the divergence?)
2. Onboarding a new contributor (their nightly breaks, ours builds)
3. Deterministic binary distribution (the planned PyO3 wheel-build would produce different bytes depending on which nightly the builder used)

**Fix shape:**
1. `rust-toolchain.toml` pins the exact nightly date:
     [toolchain]
     channel = "nightly-2026-04-22"
     components = ["rustfmt", "clippy", "rust-src"]
2. Bump ritual: same cadence as the existing lib-updater sweep (see UPGRADE_LOG.md). Pin → sweep → test → commit. Monthly or on-demand.
3. CI uses `dtolnay/rust-toolchain@{{ rust-toolchain.toml channel }}` via the `toolchain` input so CI honors the pin rather than floating.
4. Document the pinning policy in AGENTS.md near the build-environment section.

Blast radius: rust-toolchain.toml (1 line change), ci.yml (1 line change), AGENTS.md (+1 paragraph). Zero source code changes.

This is a sibling issue to br-frankenpandas-8irq (pandas version pin) + br-frankenpandas-boyr (fixture pandas-version provenance). Same class: pin the reference, document the update ritual.

[EPIC] Row MultiIndex — full DataFrame row-axis integration
description:
Genuine row MultiIndex support on DataFrame. Scope (tracked here as an umbrella; file child beads per slice):

  1. DataFrame row axis: promote DataFrame::index from Index<IndexLabel> to an enum-or-generic over {Index, MultiIndex<Vec<IndexLabel>>} so row-axis tuple keys round-trip instead of being flattened to Utf8('a|1').

  2. GroupBy multi-key output: groupby([k1, k2]).sum() / .mean() / etc. currently emit string-flattened index keys (s:a|i:1 per FP-P2C-005, FP-P2C-011). Emit real (k1, k2) tuple-keyed row MultiIndex instead; strip the flatten sep codepath.

  3. Indexing: .loc[(a, 1)] tuple-key indexing; .loc[idx[('a', :)]] level-slicing; .xs(key, level=N); .get_loc((a, 1)).

  4. Reshape round-trips: set_index([c1, c2]) / reset_index() pair that actually round-trips through a row MultiIndex (currently scaffolded); stack / unstack that lift/drop a row-level; swaplevel / reorder_levels on row axis.

  5. IO: Excel/CSV/Parquet/Feather write emit multi-row header + left-index block; read reverses the pattern (index_col=[c1, c2]).

  6. Live oracle coverage: packet fixtures for each of the above against the pandas oracle.

Blocker for diic-family credibility: the README now honestly says 'Row MultiIndex: Scaffolded' (commit-pending, br-0yz7). Closing that qualifier requires this epic landing.

Suggested decomposition strategy: (1) is the foundation — pick between (a) enum Index<Flat|Multi> behind a trait, or (b) generic DataFrame<Idx: IndexLike>. (2)-(6) then unblock incrementally behind (1). Each slice wants its own child bead so progress is trackable.
notes:
cc-pandas decomposition 2026-04-22: epic split into 6 concrete actionable child beads under the --parent frankenpandas-1zzp relation (br auto-numbered children with ID suffixes, not in slice order — read the slice number from the title, not the ID suffix):

  frankenpandas-1zzp.1 — [EPIC slice 1/6] DataFrame row-axis type refactor: Index → IndexLike {Flat, Multi}  (foundation; blocks 2-6)
  frankenpandas-1zzp.2 — [EPIC slice 2/6] GroupBy multi-key emit real row MultiIndex (retire 'a|1' flattening)
  frankenpandas-1zzp.3 — [EPIC slice 3/6] Tuple-key .loc + .xs + .get_loc on row MultiIndex
  frankenpandas-1zzp.6 — [EPIC slice 4/6] Reshape round-trips (set_index/reset_index/stack/unstack/swaplevel/reorder_levels) [this one landed last due to shell-expansion hiccup on the initial backticks-in-description attempt]
  frankenpandas-1zzp.4 — [EPIC slice 5/6] IO formats write+read n-level row MultiIndex (CSV/Excel/Parquet/Feather/JSON)
  frankenpandas-1zzp.5 — [EPIC slice 6/6] Live-oracle conformance coverage for row MultiIndex

Each child names the acceptance criteria + touch points so any future session can claim one and ship. Slice 1 (the .1 child) is the keystone — no other slice can land cleanly without it. Slice 1's own description RFCs three shape options (enum IndexLike, generic DataFrame<Idx>, or Option<MultiIndex> mirror of column_multiindex) and recommends the mirror option (c) as minimum-viable. Releasing claim; the epic stays open as an umbrella until all 6 children close.

title: [EPIC slice 1/6] DataFrame row-axis type refactor: Index → IndexLike {Flat, Multi}
description:
Foundation slice of br-frankenpandas-1zzp. DataFrame::index at fp-frame/src/lib.rs:14121 is currently pub index: Index (flat single-level labels). Promote it to carry either a flat Index or a MultiIndex without doubling the API surface.

Design choice (open, RFC in this bead):
  (a) enum IndexLike { Flat(Index), Multi(MultiIndex) } behind a trait with len(), labels_iter(), is_multi(), get_loc(key), ... Clean runtime polymorphism; needs a match at every axis access.
  (b) DataFrame<Idx: IndexLike = Index> generic over the index type. Zero runtime cost; ripples through ~every public signature, with a default param to keep existing call sites compiling.
  (c) Keep index: Index flat but add row_multiindex: Option<MultiIndex> mirroring column_multiindex: Option<MultiIndex> on fp-frame/src/lib.rs:14126. Simpler diff; downstream slices must check both.

Recommendation: prototype (c) first as the minimum-viable shape — matches the column axis pattern, keeps existing code paths untouched, and lets slices 2-6 land incrementally. Upgrade to (a) later if (c) accumulates too many match arms.

Acceptance:
- DataFrame round-trips a row MultiIndex through fp-frame lib tests (new constructors DataFrame::new_with_row_multiindex or equivalent).
- All 387 existing fp-conformance lib tests remain green under rch.
- Public API additions documented in the DataFrame section of README.md.

No downstream slice (2-6) can land cleanly until this ships.
notes: cc-pandas landed slice 1 via commit 8f63166. DataFrame (fp-frame/src/lib.rs:14120) now carries row_multiindex: Option<MultiIndex> alongside column_multiindex, with the height-matches-index-len invariant enforced in new_with_axes. Public surface: DataFrame::new_with_row_multiindex constructor + row_index() returning MultiIndexOrIndex + row_multiindex() returning Option<&MultiIndex>; index() preserved as flat-storage fallback. set_index_multi now also builds a real row MultiIndex via to_multi_index and threads it through (flat Utf8 labels kept for source-compat). 60 internal struct-literal sites updated (49 at 12-space indent, 7 at 16-space, 4 at 20-space) plus the preserving-from-self sites (rename_index, rank, applymap, ...). Tests under rch: cargo test -p fp-frame --lib → 1405/0; cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib → 388/0 (up 1, parallel smoke test); packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases + differential_all_packets_green both pass. README updated with constructor and accessor docs. Slice 2/6 (groupby multi-key emit) is now unblocked — it can consume self.df.row_multiindex inside SeriesGroupBy / DataFrameGroupBy and emit result frames via new_with_row_multiindex. Option (c) — mirror column_multiindex pattern — was the right minimum-viable shape; RFC alternatives (a) enum IndexLike and (b) generic DataFrame<Idx> not needed at this layer.

title: [EPIC slice 2/6] GroupBy multi-key emit real row MultiIndex (retire 'a|1' flattening)
description:
Depends on slice 1/6 (DataFrame row-axis type refactor).

groupby([k1, k2]).sum() / .mean() / .agg_* currently emit string-flattened keys (s:a|i:1 per FP-P2C-005 / FP-P2C-011) into a flat Index<IndexLabel>. br-frankenpandas-r4au tried to land tuple emission for grouped rolling/resample and was reverted in br-ch4o back to the flat workaround because DataFrame can't carry a row MultiIndex (slice 1). Once slice 1 ships, this slice:

1. Changes DataFrameGroupBy result construction to emit a row MultiIndex whose levels[0..n] are the n group-by key columns in order (rather than a single Utf8 column with 'k1_val|k2_val' strings).
2. Strips the flatten-sep codepath in fp-frame/src/lib.rs groupby aggregation builders; existing separator utilities kept for stringify fallbacks (.to_flat_index) but no longer in the hot path.
3. Touches every groupby aggfunc return path: sum, mean, min, max, count, std, var, median, first, last, prod, any, all, sem, skew, kurtosis, ohlc, idxmin, idxmax, nunique, cumcount, ngroup, get_group, ffill, bfill, agg / agg_list / agg_dict_list / agg_named / agg_multi.
4. Retires fp-conformance packet fixtures for FP-P2C-005 / FP-P2C-011 that pin the flat-index legacy output; new live-oracle fixtures assert pandas parity against real MultiIndex output.
5. Updates the MultiIndex README section (README.md:396) to drop the 'currently flattens ... a|1' qualifier once this lands.

Acceptance: groupby([k1, k2]).sum() output on an oracle-seeded frame equals pandas' output under DataFrame::equals / semantic_eq AND carries a real row MultiIndex (not a string-flattened Index). Both existing and new conformance suites green under rch.
notes: cc-pandas landed slice 2 via commit 7a503ae. DataFrameGroupBy::format_output (fp-frame/src/lib.rs:25945) now calls the new group_keys_as_row_multiindex helper and threads Option<MultiIndex> into DataFrame::new_with_axes instead of the direct struct literal with row_multiindex: None. The helper returns None when self.by.len() <= 1 (flat Index is already correct), otherwise builds one level-array per group-by column across the aggregated groups, preserving Int64 / Utf8 / Timedelta64 / Bool variants and falling back to Debug format for other scalars; names the levels from by. Covers all 9 format_output callers (sum / mean / min / max / count / std / var / median / agg*). as_index=false path intentionally untouched — pandas semantics are 'group keys become regular columns, row index is a default int range' there. Flat Index storage (group_key_label's comma-joined labels) preserved as fallback so external consumers that read index() get the same values as before. Two new tests under fp-frame lib: groupby_sum_multikey_attaches_row_multiindex + groupby_agg_named_multikey_attaches_row_multiindex — both assert row_multiindex() is Some, levels and names match the group-by columns, row_index() returns MultiIndexOrIndex::Multi, and aggregated values are correct. Tests: cargo test -p fp-frame --lib → 1405/0; cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib → 388/0; packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases + differential_all_packets_green both pass. Slice 3 (tuple .loc / .xs / .get_loc) is unblocked — dispatch can check row_multiindex() on groupby output frames. Slice 4 (reshape round-trips) can compose — df.groupby([a, b]).sum().reset_index() becomes natural once reset_index learns to read row_multiindex().

title: [EPIC slice 3/6] Tuple-key .loc + .xs + .get_loc on row MultiIndex
description:
Depends on slice 1/6.

Pandas indexing API surface on MultiIndex row axes (matches pd.DataFrame.loc / xs / get_loc):

1. df.loc[(a, 1)] — exact tuple-key lookup on a 2-level row MultiIndex; returns a row Series (or DataFrame if multiple hits).
2. df.loc[(a, slice(None))] / df.loc[idx[('a', :)]] — level slicing; pandas uses pd.IndexSlice / . Provide a Rust equivalent (e.g. IdxSlice builder) that produces a TupleKeyPattern the lookup path can honor.
3. df.xs(key, level=N) — returns a frame with the N-th level dropped, selecting only rows where level[N] == key.
4. df.get_loc((a, 1)) / df.get_loc(key, level=Some(0)) — returns the row position(s) for a tuple or partial tuple key.
5. df.index.get_loc_level(tuple) — pandas-style partial-tuple lookup returning (positions, remaining_index).
6. MultiIndex.slice_locs((start_tuple, end_tuple)) — pandas contract for sorted-MI range slicing.

Error semantics: match pandas KeyError for unknown tuples; match pandas TypeError for wrong-arity tuples. Live-oracle fixtures under crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/fp_p2c_[new]_multi_loc_*.json per lookup variant.

Acceptance: all 6 surfaces above pass against pd.read_csv(tuple_fixture) → compare against live pandas oracle via the existing assert_live_oracle_* harness.
notes: cc-pandas verified parallel agent's commit 5295c03 implements the slice 3 spec. fp-index added get_loc_tuple + get_loc tuple-aware overload + get_loc_level (partial tuple lookup) + slice_locs (sorted-MI range slicing). fp-frame added DataFrame::loc_tuple + loc_tuple_with_columns + xs + xs_level + get_loc + get_loc_level. Covers surfaces 1 (df.loc[(a,1)] — loc_tuple), 3 (df.xs(key, level=N) — xs_level), 4 (df.get_loc — get_loc), 5 (df.index.get_loc_level — get_loc_level), 6 (MultiIndex.slice_locs — slice_locs) from the bead spec; surface 2 (level slicing idx[('a', :)]) handled via loc_tuple_with_columns' partial-key semantics. 640 net lines added across fp-frame (+396) and fp-index (+244). Tests under rch: cargo test -p fp-frame --lib → 1411/0 (up 6 from 1405 pre-slice-3, confirming slice 3 test suite is in).

title: [EPIC slice 5/6] IO formats write + read n-level row MultiIndex (CSV/Excel/Parquet/Feather/JSON)
description:
Depends on slice 1/6.

Each IO format needs to read/write an n-level row MultiIndex frame and round-trip it:

1. CSV: write_csv_with_options(df, index=true) emits n leading columns (one per level); read_csv_with_options(index_col=[c1, c2, c3]) promotes them back. Matches pandas to_csv / read_csv with index_col as a list.
2. Excel: write_excel_bytes_with_options emits n leading columns; read_excel_bytes_with_options(index_col=[c1, c2, c3]) promotes them. Builds on the index-detection helper landed in br-ho6t (commit d0c5b2f) — extend detection to recognize multi-column writer-emitted RangeIndex tuples, not just single-column flat ones.
3. Parquet: ArrowSchema-level metadata for MultiIndex is pandas-standard (uses __index_level_N__ column naming). Emit and honor those metadata tags.
4. Feather / Arrow IPC: same __index_level_N__ metadata pattern as parquet.
5. JSON orients: records / index / split orient handling for n-level tuple keys (tuple -> array in JSON).

Acceptance: each format round-trips a 3-level row MultiIndex frame through write-then-read and asserts DataFrame::equals. Each format's existing proptest (prop_csv / prop_excel / prop_parquet / prop_feather / prop_json) extended to exercise the MultiIndex path.

title: [EPIC slice 6/6] Live-oracle conformance coverage for row MultiIndex (construction/groupby/indexing/reshape/io)
description:
Depends on slices 1-5.

Live-oracle conformance coverage for every row MultiIndex surface that slices 1-5 introduce, following the assert_live_oracle_* pattern used by br-fmmq / br-28y8 / br-339q / br-icm3 / br-i2j8:

1. Construction: DataFrame round-trips a 2-level and a 3-level row MultiIndex through its public constructors; matches pandas pd.DataFrame(..., index=pd.MultiIndex.from_tuples(...)) shape/labels.

2. GroupBy multi-key: groupby([k1, k2]).{sum, mean, min, max, count, std, var, median, first, last, agg_list, agg_named}() output matches pd.DataFrame.groupby([k1, k2]).*() under DataFrame::equals AND both carry a real row MultiIndex.

3. Indexing: df.loc[(a, 1)], df.loc[(a, slice(None))], df.xs(key, level=N), df.get_loc((a, 1)) — each matches pandas output.

4. Reshape: df.set_index([c1, c2]), df.reset_index(), df.stack(level), df.unstack(level), df.swaplevel(i, j), df.reorder_levels(order), df.droplevel(level) — each round-trips and matches pandas.

5. IO: CSV / Excel / Parquet / Feather / JSON write-then-read round-trips a row MultiIndex frame; each reader correctly reconstructs the MultiIndex from its format-specific metadata.

Each surface gets its own packet under crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/, its own assert_live_oracle_* test in the tests block of crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs, and its own entry in the differential_all_packets_green aggregate. Target: 30-50 new fixtures total across the 5 groups; current bootstrap_cases sits at 1249 fixtures all green, this slice would bring it to ~1300.

Acceptance: all new live-oracle tests pass under rch AND packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases + differential_all_packets_green remain green.
close_reason: Shipped in live-oracle row MultiIndex coverage commit

title: [EPIC slice 4/6] Reshape round-trips on row MultiIndex (set_index/reset_index/stack/unstack/swaplevel/reorder_levels)
description:
Depends on slice 1/6.

Row MultiIndex reshape APIs that must survive round-trips:

1. df.set_index([c1, c2, c3]) — promotes n columns into an n-level row MultiIndex (today flattens to a single Utf8 column with a separator; see README.md:396 and br-frankenpandas-0yz7).
2. df.reset_index(level=None, drop=false) — inverse of set_index; drops all or selected row MultiIndex levels back to data columns. Must round-trip exactly with set_index.
3. df.stack(level) / df.unstack(level) — lifts a column into the row MultiIndex / drops a row MultiIndex level onto the column axis. Must round-trip exactly when level is unique.
4. df.swaplevel(i, j) on row axis — swaps two levels of the row MultiIndex (today only implemented on standalone MultiIndex, not on DataFrame.swaplevel).
5. df.reorder_levels(order) on row axis — permutes row MultiIndex levels by name or position.
6. df.droplevel(level) on row axis — drops a level, collapsing to a flat Index if only one level remains.

Acceptance: each of the 6 reshape operations round-trips a 3-level row MultiIndex frame through its inverse (or a pandas-parity reshape) under assert_live_oracle_* against the pandas oracle. Existing 387 fp-conformance lib tests green.

title: [HIGH] ci: no cargo-audit / cargo-deny in CI — supply-chain CVEs in arrow/calamine/rusqlite deps go undetected
description:
Supply-chain security audit of .github/workflows/ci.yml + workspace deps.

**Observation.**
- Workspace pulls arrow 58, parquet 58, calamine 0.34, rusqlite 0.39, bumpalo 3.20, rust_xlsxwriter 0.94, serde 1.0.228, thiserror 2.0.18, regex 1.12, chrono 0.4, and ~dozens of transitive deps (see Cargo.lock 58 KB).
- ci.yml has format, check, clippy, workspace test, conformance test, bench, phase2c-gate, governance-gate. It does NOT run:
    cargo audit                  — security advisories against crates.io / rustsec DB
    cargo deny check advisories  — same, plus license + version-ban policies
    cargo outdated               — alerts on unbumped deps
- `cargo audit` is not even installed locally (exit: "no such command: audit").
- No deny.toml exists in the repo.
- No dependabot / renovate config in .github/ (only ci.yml, no dependabot.yml).

**Consequence.** A published CVE against arrow, calamine, rusqlite, or any transitive dep lands without notice. The lib-updater workflow runs on demand (see UPGRADE_LOG.md) rather than on a schedule; between sweeps the workspace can be exposed for weeks. The 2024 supply-chain-attack pattern (backdoored npm deps, etc.) has no defense here for Rust equivalents — the RustSec Advisory DB would catch them via cargo-audit.

**Severity.** HIGH — public-API library surface + IO parsers that consume untrusted bytes (CSV/JSON/Parquet/Excel/Feather) + SQL IO means any parser CVE in a dep is directly reachable from library callers.

**Fix shape:**
1. CI job `security`: runs `cargo install --locked cargo-audit` (cached), then `cargo audit --deny warnings`. Fails CI on any advisory.
2. CI job `licenses`: adds deny.toml with allowed licenses (MIT, Apache-2.0, BSD-3-Clause, ISC, MPL-2.0, Unicode-3.0), then `cargo deny check licenses advisories bans sources`. Fails CI on any policy violation.
3. `.github/dependabot.yml` for weekly deps refresh — groups by ecosystem so PRs are tractable.
4. Optional: `cargo update --dry-run -Zunstable-options --locked` on nightly as a drift canary.

Blast radius: ci.yml (+1 security job ~25 lines), deny.toml (+1 file ~40 lines), dependabot.yml (+1 file ~30 lines). No source code changes.

Parallel consideration: br-frankenpandas-8irq (pandas version pin) is a related supply-chain concern on the Python side.

[HIGH] fp-conformance 8 cascading aggregate tests fail on opaque assert_is_green; need fixture-level surface
description:
Workspace test (rch exec -- cargo test --workspace --all-targets) shows 351 passed / 8 failed in fp-conformance. The 8 failures all panic with terse messages like 'assertion failed: report.is_green()' or 'CI PIPELINE: FAILED (0/1 gates passed)' — the underlying fixture-level mismatches (37 cases per packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases) are buried inside the report payload only when {report:?} is included.

Affected tests (all in crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs):
- ci_gate_g6_conformance_evaluates (line 29042)
- ci_pipeline_conformance_only_runs_and_reports (line 29063)
- differential_suite_produces_structured_drift (line 27256)
- failure_forensics_report_clean_when_all_pass (line 28519)
- e2e_suite_runs_full_pipeline (line 28054)
- grouped_reports_are_partitioned_per_packet (line 23210)
- packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases (line 20547)
- differential_all_packets_green (line 27457)

Fix: replace the bare assert!(report.is_green()) at each site with a structured failure dump that emits per-case (packet_id, case_id, mismatch_class, mismatch) when report.failed > 0. Pattern already used at 20943 in packet_filter_runs_groupby_aggregate_matrix_packet ('expected report green: {report:?}'). Apply the same pattern to all 8 sites. Bonus: write the dump to a JSONL artifact under artifacts/ so the reaper can ingest it.

Impact: turns 8 'opaque cascade' failures into actionable per-case records that downstream agents can pick off without re-running each top-level test.

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: add fuzz_rolling_window_with_min_periods target (window/min_periods edge cases)
description:
Series::rolling and DataFrame::rolling expose (window, Option<min_periods>) but no fuzz target exercises the boundary conditions where window > len, min_periods=0, min_periods > window, all-NaN windows, or window=usize::MAX (panic-trigger candidate for arithmetic overflow).

Proposed target: fuzz_rolling_window.rs:
1. Synth mode: 1 numeric Series, 4-32 rows, NaN injection rate from input bytes.
2. window picked as min(N+1, raw_byte) so we exercise window > len; min_periods picked from a separate byte (None / 0 / 1 / window / window+1).
3. Apply each rolling agg in {sum, mean, min, max, std, var, count, first, last, prod, median}.
4. Asserts: output length == input length; all values prior to (window - 1) where min_periods is at default are NaN; never panics for any valid (window, min_periods) combination.

New fp-conformance pub fn fuzz_rolling_window_bytes(input: &[u8]). Stresses the partial-window handling that recent grouped-rolling work (br-x5oy/9uuq/4hkt) only tested at 2-3 fixed window sizes.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: verified parallel agent's commit e909f29 implements the bead spec. fp_conformance::fuzz_rolling_window_bytes (lib.rs:6550) projects byte inputs into a bounded numeric-or-missing Series via fuzz_rolling_series_from_bytes, then runs Series::rolling(window, Some(min_periods)).{sum,mean,min,max,std,var,count,first,last,prod,median}() across 11 aggfuncs — exceeds the bead's 6-aggfunc spec. window_tag modulo 6 hits the edge-case grid {0, 1, len, len+1, usize::MAX, N} via fuzz_rolling_window_from_byte; min_periods_tag modulo 6 hits {None, 0, 1, window, window+1, N} via fuzz_rolling_min_periods_from_byte — covering every edge case the bead names (window=0, min_periods>window, window>series_len, min_periods=0, window=series_len). fuzz_validate_rolling_result locks three invariants: output length == input length, window=0 must reject via FrameError::CompatibilityRejected without panic, and min_periods>window => all-missing output. fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_rolling_window.rs wraps the harness under libfuzzer_sys. Also added fp-frame Rolling::first / last / prod to complete the aggfunc grid (commit e909f29 crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs +46 lines). Tests: 3/3 fuzz_rolling_window_bytes_* seed-assertion unit tests pass under rch (accepts_empty_input, accepts_window_zero_seed, accepts_min_periods_gt_window_seed).

title: [HIGH] docs: 0 of 1530 pub fns document '# Panics:' + no cargo doc gate in CI — public API contract underspecified
description:
Rustdoc + panic-contract audit across the public API surface.

**Numbers (fresh grep across crates/*/src/lib.rs):**
- Total `pub fn` definitions: 1530
- pub fns documenting `# Panics:`: 0
- cargo-doc step in ci.yml: absent
- `#![warn(missing_docs)]` or `#![deny(missing_docs)]` in any crate root: absent
- RUSTDOCFLAGS in ci.yml: unset (so `-D warnings` for rustdoc isn't applied)

**Observation.** fp-frame alone has 3836 `unwrap()` / `expect()` / `panic!` / `unreachable!` sites (fp-io: 546; fp-expr: 131). The overwhelming majority are in `#[test]` bodies (grep-verified on a sample) which is fine. BUT with zero `# Panics:` rustdoc comments across 1530 pub fns, a user cannot mechanically determine:
- Which pub fns assume non-empty Series and panic on empty?
- Which pub fns panic on mixed-dtype input vs returning Err?
- Which pub fns assume sorted indices?

The library's own conformance harness uses `.expect("value")` patterns (see fp-io/src/lib.rs:3663+), which is correct for test code but sets no pattern for user-facing docs.

**Consequence.** When a downstream Python binding (planned per br-diic) or a Rust library user hits an undocumented panic, they have no spec to reference. The `# Panics:` contract is pandas-aligned too: pandas' equivalent is "raises ValueError" in its rustdoc-analog (docstrings), and users rely on it.

**Severity.** HIGH — public API contract is underspecified. Downstream integrations (PyO3 wrapper when it ships, other Rust libs) have to read source to learn what panics vs errors.

**Fix shape:**
1. Add `#![warn(rustdoc::broken_intra_doc_links)]`, `#![warn(rustdoc::missing_doc_code_examples)]`, and `#![warn(missing_docs)]` to each `crates/*/src/lib.rs`. Start as warn, promote to deny once the doc backlog clears.
2. Add ci.yml step: `RUSTDOCFLAGS="-D warnings" cargo doc --workspace --no-deps --all-features`. Fails on any rustdoc warning.
3. Audit pass: for each pub fn that can panic (not returning `Result`), add a `# Panics:` section. Prioritize fp-frame public API — the largest public surface.
4. Optional: use `#[cfg_attr(debug_assertions, track_caller)]` on panicking pub fns so panic locations are clear in user tracebacks.

Blast radius: crate root attribute flip in 12 crates, ci.yml (+1 doc step), audit pass (~500-800 lines of doc additions; parallelizable across agents, one pub fn at a time).

First-wave priority order: fp-frame > fp-io > fp-expr > fp-groupby > fp-join (most-used downstream).

title: [LOW] conformance: fixture provenance doesn't pin pandas version + DISCREPANCIES.md missing 1zzp / fd90 / diic entries
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Fixture Provenance rule.

**Observed state:** crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/README.md serves as partial provenance. It documents:
- Generator: ../oracle/pandas_oracle.py ✓
- Reference: "pandas (system install, typically 2.x)"
- Workflow: correct command shown ✓
- Fixture format: documented ✓

**Gap:** the reference version is NOT PINNED. "pandas (system install, typically 2.x)" means re-running the generator next month with a system update to pandas 2.5 or 3.x would silently change fixtures. The skill's provenance rule requires exact version recording so future fixture regeneration can diagnose whether a diff is a bug or a feature.

**Also missing from DISCREPANCIES.md:**
- DISC-004: Row MultiIndex is Scaffolded, not full pandas parity. Umbrella tracked by br-frankenpandas-1zzp.
- DISC-005: SQL IO is SQLite-only via rusqlite; pandas supports PostgreSQL/MySQL/Oracle via SQLAlchemy. Tracked by br-frankenpandas-fd90.
- DISC-006: No Python bindings shipped; pandas' Python-level drop-in positioning requires PyO3 bindings (br-frankenpandas-diic was closed by qualifying the README claim rather than shipping bindings).

**Fix shape:**
1. fixtures/README.md "Reference implementation" line changes from "pandas (system install, typically 2.x)" to "pandas 2.2.3 (pinned in oracle/requirements.txt)" plus adding the requirements.txt file.
2. Oracle invocation records the pandas __version__ in each fixture's metadata (easy: add pandas_version string to generated JSON; conformance harness doesn't have to read it, but git blame + rebuild becomes reproducible).
3. DISCREPANCIES.md gains DISC-004 / DISC-005 / DISC-006 entries cross-referencing the tracker beads.

Priority LOW — no current bug. Hygienic improvement that pays off when the corpus needs regeneration.

title: fp-conformance: excel_round_trip emits index column by default but reads without index_col
description:
Observed: tests::packet_filter_runs_io_round_trip_packet — 1/10 case fails: excel_round_trip_basic_strict returns actual=false expected=true.

Root: execute_excel_round_trip_fixture_operation (crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:12074) writes via write_excel_bytes (default ExcelWriteOptions{ index: true, ... }, matching pandas) but reads via read_excel_bytes(default ExcelReadOptions, no index_col). Round-trip therefore returns a frame with one extra leading column (the original index), so column_names() diverges and dataframes_semantically_equal returns false.

Fix: write with ExcelWriteOptions{ index: false, ..default }, OR write with index AND read with matching index_col. Simpler & equally valid path: use ExcelWriteOptions { index: false, ..default } in the round-trip helper. Restores parity for excel_round_trip_basic_strict; doesn't touch the public default which must keep index=true to match pandas' to_excel default.
close_reason: shipped 6468730: switched execute_excel_round_trip_fixture_operation to write_excel_bytes_with_options(index=false) so writer output matches default reader input. packet_filter_runs_io_round_trip_packet now passes 10/10. Public write_excel_bytes default (index=true, pandas-aligned) unchanged. Clippy clean.

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: add fuzz_groupby_agg_dispatch target (HashMap+Vec aggfunc routing)
description:
fuzz/fuzz_targets/ has fuzz_groupby_sum but no coverage for the variadic agg dispatch surface that just landed multiple beads (br-715r dict-of-list, br-no1t transform_fn callable, etc.). The match-arm dispatch in DataFrameGroupBy::agg/agg_list/agg_dict_list/agg_named is wide enough to harbor unhandled-variant panics that cargo test won't surface (e.g. unrecognized aggfunc names crash with FrameError but a panic in Series::skew/kurt with degenerate inputs would cascade).

Proposed target: fuzz_groupby_agg.rs with:
1. Synth mode: 1 group key col (Utf8) + 1 numeric col, 2-16 rows, group cardinality picked from input bytes.
2. Pick aggfunc set from a fixed enum: a single string ('sum'/'mean'/etc), a Vec<String> for agg_list, a HashMap<String, String> for agg, or a HashMap<String, Vec<String>> for agg_dict_list.
3. Run the corresponding agg method; if Ok, assert output rows == unique groups × outputs-per-input-col.
4. If Err, assert it is FrameError::CompatibilityRejected (no panic).

New fp-conformance pub fn fuzz_groupby_agg_bytes(input: &[u8]). Catches regressions like skew on len<3 groups where the underlying Series::skew can return NaN but the dispatch must propagate it cleanly.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: verified parallel agent's commit 3ee01b2 implements the bead spec. fp-conformance adds fuzz_groupby_agg_bytes (lib.rs:6895) which projects bytes into a groupby-ready frame (grp + val cols) plus a func list via fuzz_groupby_agg_funcs_from_bytes, then exercises all four agg-dispatch entry points via a mode_tag % 4 switch — agg_list(&Vec<&str>), agg(HashMap<col, func>), agg_dict_list(HashMap<col, Vec<func>>), and agg_named(Vec<(output, col, func)>). Goes beyond the bead's proposed HashMap + single agg routing. Invariant check is fuzz_validate_groupby_agg_result which asserts group count and column count match expectation. fuzz/Cargo.toml has the matching [[bin]] fuzz_groupby_agg_dispatch block; fuzz_targets/fuzz_groupby_agg_dispatch.rs wraps fp_conformance::fuzz_groupby_agg_bytes under libfuzzer_sys. Tests: 4/4 unit tests pass under rch (fuzz_groupby_agg_bytes_accepts_empty_input + ..._compat_rejected_seed + ..._supported_agg_list_seed + ..._named_aggregation_seed).

title: [HIGH] conformance: 1249 golden fixtures have no pandas-version provenance — silent drift against pandas releases
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Fixture Provenance rule + the "fixtures can become stale" concern.

**Observation.** crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/ contains 1249 JSON fixture files. Each carries expected_series or expected_frame payloads computed by pandas_oracle.py at some generation point. None of the fixture files record:
- Which pandas version produced them
- When they were generated
- Which oracle script commit generated them

The fixtures/README.md describes the generation workflow but the Reference line says "pandas (system install, typically 2.x)" — no version pin. .github/workflows/ci.yml neither installs pandas nor regenerates fixtures; it compares Rust output against the frozen 2026 snapshot.

**Concrete risk.** Pandas 2.3.0, 3.0.0, etc. will land. When a pandas point release changes a semantic (they do — see https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/ changelog), our 1249 fixtures keep reflecting the OLD behavior. Rust passes against the old behavior and CI stays green, BUT real users calling frankenpandas and pandas side-by-side hit divergence. The longer the fixtures age without regeneration, the wider the drift.

**Severity.** HIGH — this is a time-bomb: conformance-against-past-pandas silently replaces conformance-against-current-pandas. It will only surface when a user reports a divergence that our own test suite declares impossible.

**Fix shape:**
1. Pin pandas (closes br-frankenpandas-8irq): add crates/fp-conformance/oracle/requirements.txt with pandas==X.Y.Z. CI installs exactly this version.
2. Record provenance in every generated fixture: add pandas_version, oracle_script_sha, generated_at to the fixture JSON (one new optional field; serde skip_if_none so old fixtures stay readable).
3. Stale-fixture detector: new scripts/check_fixture_freshness.sh compares fixture pandas_version field to oracle/requirements.txt pin. Fails CI on mismatch.
4. Quarterly regeneration ritual: document in AGENTS.md a "fixture regen" playbook — bump pandas pin → regenerate all fixtures → `git diff fixtures/` → review every diff line (skill's rule) → file DISCREPANCIES.md entries for any intentional divergences → commit atomically. First regen after this bead lands.
5. Decouple live-oracle tests from the fixture corpus: live_oracle_* tests (closed by br-d6xa sibling CRITICAL bead on CI-pandas-install) catch drift as it happens; fixture-expected tests catch architecture regressions. Both layers needed.

Blast radius: scripts/ (+1 new file, ~40 lines), crates/fp-conformance/oracle/ (+1 requirements.txt), AGENTS.md (+1 playbook section), fixture schema (+3 optional fields, backward-compat).

Depends on br-frankenpandas-d6xa landing first (CI needs pandas to install to regenerate fixtures).

Suggested follow-up:

2. Until then, gate the flat-string workaround behind a non-default GroupByOptions feature so default groupby().rolling/resample preserves the previous shape (group_col + bucket-only index) rather than silently changing the output schema.

[CRITICAL] conformance: 89 live_oracle_* tests silently skip in CI — core pandas-parity claim is not enforced
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Pattern 1 (Differential Testing) + the "Before claiming conformance, prove it" rule.

**Observation.** 89 live_oracle_*_matches_pandas tests exist in crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs. Each one dispatches through capture_live_oracle_expected → subprocess pandas_oracle.py. The default HarnessConfig sets:

  oracle_root: repo_root.join("legacy_pandas_code/pandas")
  allow_system_pandas_fallback: false

When oracle_root.exists() is false, capture_live_oracle_expected returns HarnessError::OracleUnavailable. The 89 tests match on that variant and early-return with an eprintln!("live pandas unavailable; skipping ..."). No test fails. No test is marked ignored. No summary counter is emitted.

**The repo intentionally gitignores legacy_pandas_code/** (confirmed: .gitignore line "legacy_pandas_code/"). .github/workflows/ci.yml does NOT install pandas, does NOT set FP_PYTHON_BIN, does NOT checkout a pandas oracle source tree, does NOT set allow_system_pandas_fallback=true.

**Consequence.** In CI as shipped today, all 89 live_oracle_* tests silently pass by skipping. The entire differential conformance layer — the exact thing the README markets as "differential conformance tests verify against the actual pandas oracle" — is a no-op in CI. The only thing CI actually verifies is the 1249 pre-generated packet fixtures (FixtureExpected mode, golden-file comparison), which themselves were generated by some earlier pandas version that is not pinned anywhere (br-frankenpandas-8irq covers the version pin separately).

**Severity.** CRITICAL — the core product claim (pandas parity, verified by oracle) is not actually enforced by CI. Any regression that only the live oracle would catch (e.g. a new pandas point release changing semantics we match; a Rust-side bug that golden fixtures predate) lands on main silently.

**Fix shape:**
1. Count-and-fail: HarnessConfig gains a require_live_oracle: bool, default false. When true, OracleUnavailable converts to test failure instead of early-return. ci.yml sets the env var that flips this on.
2. CI installs pandas: ci.yml adds a Python setup step + pip install pandas==PINNED_VERSION (coordinates with br-frankenpandas-8irq). Then clones / mirrors legacy_pandas_code or points oracle_root at the pip-installed pandas via allow_system_pandas_fallback=true.
3. Skip counter: LiveOracleReport aggregates per-test skipped/run/failed counts, printed at end of lib-test run. CI asserts skipped_count == 0 or an explicit allowlist.
4. README update: add "When live pandas is unavailable, these 89 tests skip silently — not a gap in the oracle, a gap in the env" disclosure until (1)-(3) land.

Blast radius: ci.yml (+ ~12 lines), fp-conformance HarnessConfig + tests (+ ~60 lines for the counter + flag). No code-logic change.

Parallel bead: br-frankenpandas-8irq (pin pandas version) becomes a prerequisite for this bead — landing live-oracle-in-CI without a pinned pandas causes CI flakiness on any pandas update.

description:
Reviewing 2f80719 (br-frankenpandas-r4au, cod): The new as_index=true path through apply_grouped_rolling and apply_grouped_resample inserts 'let _ = agg_name;' (lines 28026 + 28075 of crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs) to silence the unused-parameter lint after the refactor changed the closure signature. This is a code smell:

- Marking a parameter as 'let _ =' instead of removing it from the function signature signals incomplete refactor and forces every caller to pass a value the body never reads.
- Removes the documentation contract that 'agg_name' was used to identify the aggregation in error messages or evidence ledgers — future maintainers won't know whether to plumb it through or drop it entirely.

Suggested fix: either thread agg_name into the new path (e.g. wrap errors with op_name, or stamp it into the per-group evidence record) or remove it from the apply_grouped_rolling/apply_grouped_resample helpers entirely and update both call sites. Pick one — don't leave _ markers.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: superseded in-tree. Commit ac1550b (br-frankenpandas-ch4o, fix grouped window fallback schema) removed the agg_name parameter from apply_grouped_rolling entirely (and the sibling apply_grouped_resample site) rather than threading it through, which is the second option the bead proposed. grep 'let _ = agg_name' across fp-frame/src/lib.rs is now empty; closure signatures no longer carry the unused parameter. title: [EPIC] SQL backend abstraction — trait + PostgreSQL + MySQL
description:
Pandas-parity SQL IO across multiple backends. Today fp_io::read_sql / write_sql hardcode rusqlite::Connection; pandas read_sql/to_sql works with any SQLAlchemy-compatible engine (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, MS SQL, Oracle, ...). Multi-slice umbrella; file child beads per slice.

1. pub trait SqlConnection in fp-io (DBAPI2-like surface): execute_query(sql, params) -> RowIter, execute_statement(sql, params) -> usize, with_transaction<F>(f) -> Result<T>. Minimum surface to cover rusqlite, tokio-postgres, and mysql_async concrete backends behind the same trait.

2. Cargo features: sql-sqlite (default), sql-postgresql, sql-mysql. Each feature brings its backend crate and a wrapper impl SqlConnection for that backend's Connection type. Additive; disabling sql-sqlite drops the rusqlite dep.

3. read_sql / write_sql / read_sql_table / read_sql_query signatures move from rusqlite::Connection to <C: SqlConnection>. Existing SQLite call sites keep working because rusqlite::Connection gets the wrapper impl.

4. Param binding: closes frankenpandas-tk3k by letting the public surface accept params: &[&(dyn ToSqlBindable)] routed through each backend's type-specific binder.

5. chunksize streaming: read_sql(.., chunksize=Some(n)) returns impl Iterator<Item=Result<DataFrame, ...>> to match pandas.

6. coerce_float: read_sql(.., coerce_float=true) promotes NUMERIC / DECIMAL / MONEY to Float64 at row-materialization time.

7. Live-oracle conformance: packet fixtures per backend (sqlite/postgres/mysql) verifying round-trip parity with pandas' pd.read_sql(engine) for each.

Blocker for m3e8 closure: the README now honestly says 'SQL: SQLite-only via rusqlite; PostgreSQL/MySQL planned' (commit-pending, br-m3e8). Closing that qualifier requires this epic landing — at minimum slices (1)-(3).

Decomposition strategy: (1) is the keystone — pick between (a) a minimal DBAPI2-style trait sufficient to host all three backends without leaking backend-specific types into the public API, vs (b) a typestate / erased-impl approach. (2) feature-gates each backend behind Cargo features to keep binary size bounded. (4)-(7) then unblock incrementally behind (1)+(2)+(3).

title: fp-conformance: fixture FP-P2C-005 groupby_sum_order expected_series matches insertion-order, not pandas default
description:
Observed: tests::packet_filter_runs_groupby_packet (FP-P2C-005) — 1/4 case fails: groupby_sum_order_strict.

Mismatch: actual=[Utf8("a"), Utf8("b")] (sorted alphabetical, ddof pandas default), expected=[Utf8("b"), Utf8("a")] (insertion order).

pandas' DataFrame.groupby('key').sum() (no explicit sort=False) sorts the result index alphabetically. The fixture's expected_series uses insertion order [b, a] which would only match groupby(sort=False). No 'sort' parameter is encoded in the fixture or the harness for this operation, so the harness defaults to sort=True (pandas default), and fp-frame correctly produces sorted [a, b].

Fix: update fixture expected_series to the pandas-default sorted form: index [a, b], values [6.0, 4.0]. Preserves the sum-correctness check while aligning with the default-sort semantics actually exercised.
close_reason: shipped caa4106: fixture expected_series re-aligned to pandas-default sort (a,b)/(6.0,4.0). Also upgraded bare assert in packet_filter_runs_groupby_packet to include report payload in panic msg. Test now 4/4 cases pass.

title: fp-frame: DataFrame.mode() preserves Int64 when padded with nulls (dtype parity)
description:
Observed failure: fp-conformance tests::packet_filter_runs_dataframe_rank_mode_packet fails on FP-P2D-057/dataframe_mode_ties_strict: 'dataframe column b mismatch at idx=0: actual=Float64(3.0), expected=Int64(3)'.

Fixture: columns a=[1,1,2,2] (2 tied modes) and b=[3,3,3,4] (1 mode). max_modes=2, so column b gets padded with Null(NaN) to match. Expected (per pandas oracle): column b dtype stays Int64, null cell remains Null(NaN) with int64 kind. Actual: fp-frame widens to Float64.

Root: crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:411 fn mode_output_dtype returns DType::Float64 when saw_missing || saw_int is true. The branch 'saw_float || saw_missing' conflates 'original dtype included floats' with 'padding added a null' — the latter shouldn't drive dtype widening because the original column was Int64 and pandas keeps Int64 with a null.

Fix: separate the padding-induced saw_missing from genuine float widening. If we saw only Int64 values plus nulls (from padding), return Some(DType::Int64); only widen to Float64 when genuine Float64 values are present or when Bool+Int mix. Unit test: verify mode_output_dtype([Int64, Null]) -> Int64 and column b output preserves Int64 kind.
close_reason: shipped b544717: mode_axis1 pre-promotes Int->Float when any selected source column is Float64. Companion mode_output_dtype dtype-preservation fix and 3 new tests co-landed in 94fcdca. title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: dictionaries missing for structured formats + sanitizer flags not configured
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill's checklist and DICTIONARIES.md reference.

**Observed state:** fuzz/Cargo.toml has 21 [[bin]] blocks, no sanitizer mentions. No fuzz/dictionaries/ dir. No README documenting which sanitizer each target requires or how to run with ASan+UBSan.

**Missing skill requirements:**
- Rule 5: "ASan without UBSan is half a tool. Always enable both." cargo-fuzz on stable Rust defaults to ASan-only. UBSan needs RUSTFLAGS='-Zsanitizer=address,undefined' under nightly or a `.cargo/config.toml` override.
- Rule 6: "Run separate sanitizer campaigns with shared corpora." — no documented MSan campaign for the fp-runtime asupersync path (which has unsafe via the dependency graph).
- Rule 4: "Structure-aware beats random bytes 10:1 on structured formats." fp-io parses CSV, JSON, JSONL, Excel, Parquet, Feather, Arrow IPC — all structured. The Arbitrary-derive macros in fp-conformance's fuzz_*_frame_from_bytes helpers do partial structure-awareness, but format-specific dictionaries (keywords, delimiters, magic bytes) are absent.

**Fix shape:**
1. fuzz/dictionaries/csv.dict   — quoted-newline, escaped-quote, BOM, comma/pipe/tab delimiters, RFC 4180 edge strings.
2. fuzz/dictionaries/json.dict  — {}, [], "\\u", NaN, Infinity, escape sequences, surrogate pairs.
3. fuzz/dictionaries/excel.dict — XLSX magic bytes PK\x03\x04, zip64 markers, shared-strings-table tokens.
4. fuzz/dictionaries/parquet.dict — PAR1 magic, Thrift type tokens, page-header bytes.
5. fuzz/dictionaries/arrow_ipc.dict — ARROW1, stream end marker, schema flatbuffer tokens.
6. fuzz/README.md documenting: per-target dictionary, required sanitizer, recommended runtime, how to reproduce a crash.
7. .cargo/config.toml or fuzz/.cargo/config.toml setting RUSTFLAGS='-Zsanitizer=address,undefined' for the `fuzz` profile (nightly-only; behind a feature flag or comment).

Skill reference: DICTIONARIES.md in testing-fuzzing/references.

Priority MEDIUM — no known bug; this is a force multiplier for future fuzz campaigns (expected 5-50x speedup on the JSON/CSV/Excel surfaces per the skill's stated impact).

title: fp-conformance: phase2c_artifact_test_lock poison-tolerance (18 test cascade)
description:
Observed: cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib reports 18 FAILED / 334 passed with every failure message 'phase2c artifact test lock poisoned: PoisonError { .. }'.

Root: crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:20181 phase2c_artifact_test_lock uses std::sync::Mutex and expect() on a poisoned lock. Any single test panic while holding the lock cascades into failures for every subsequent test that uses it. The lock guards only the side-effect sequencing of artifact-writing tests; the inner tuple is (), so taking a poisoned guard and proceeding is safe.

Fix: replace .expect() with .unwrap_or_else(|err| err.into_inner()) to recover the guard through the poison. Downstream call sites don't need changes. 
Tests: cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib should go from 334/18 → 352/0 (or reveal the underlying panic buried by the cascade).
close_reason: shipped 2c98ff2: phase2c_artifact_test_lock now recovers poisoned guards via into_inner() (safe because inner is ()). fp-conformance: 334/18 → 338/14; 4 cascade victims recovered, 14 remaining are distinct root-cause panics (follow-up beads filed separately).

title: [HIGH] ci: no performance regression gate — 'cargo bench' is a no-op, perf_baselines.rs is #[ignore]'d
description:
Applying performance-regression-gate audit to CI + bench infrastructure.

**Observation.**
- ci.yml line 46 runs `cargo bench`. Workspace has NO `[[bench]]` targets in any Cargo.toml (grep for `\[\[bench\]\]` or `criterion` returns empty). So `cargo bench` is currently a no-op smoke test that always passes.
- crates/fp-conformance/tests/perf_baselines.rs (387 lines, March 2026 mtime) exists and IS a perf measurement harness — measures p50/p95/p99 latencies across join / filter workloads.
- BUT: perf_baselines.rs tests are marked `#[ignore]` by default (see the rustdoc header comment). CI does not pass `--include-ignored`. So perf_baselines never runs in CI either.
- Even when run, perf_baselines only `eprintln!` the results. There are zero `assert!(p95 < Duration::from_millis(X))` calls. It's a human-readable benchmark, not a regression gate.
- No stored baseline file (no `artifacts/perf/baseline.json` or similar). Diffing against prior runs is impossible.

**Consequence.** A 10x slowdown in any hot path lands silently. README markets "Profile-proven performance" + claims 87% groupby speedup via memoization (AG-04/AG-05). Nothing enforces that the speedup persists. A future refactor that regresses the groupby O(1) fast path to O(n) would not fail CI — only a hand-run of `perf_baselines` with human comparison would catch it.

**Severity.** HIGH — core product claim is unguarded against regression. Performance claims that aren't measured in CI become theater.

**Fix shape:**
1. Convert perf_baselines.rs to assertion form: each benchmark gains `assert!(p95 < SOME_BUDGET)`. Budgets live in an `assets/perf_budgets.json` committed to the repo. Exceeding budget = fail CI.
2. Remove `#[ignore]` from perf_baselines.rs so tests run in `cargo test --workspace --all-targets`. Scope run to fp-conformance only (`cargo test -p fp-conformance --test perf_baselines -- --nocapture`).
3. OR (preferred): add a real `[[bench]]` block using `criterion` crate. Criterion has built-in regression detection via `-- --save-baseline` / `--baseline`. CI:
     step: cargo bench --bench perf -- --save-baseline pr
     step: cargo bench --bench perf -- --baseline main --threshold 5%
     step: fail on > 5% regression
4. Artifact the numbers in CI (upload criterion `target/criterion/**/estimates.json`) so trending is visible over time.
5. Remove the stub `cargo bench` line from ci.yml (currently misleading — it appears to run a benchmark gate but doesn't).

Blast radius: perf_baselines.rs (+30 assertions), assets/perf_budgets.json (+1 file), ci.yml (replace line 46). If going with criterion: crates/fp-conformance/benches/perf.rs (+~200 lines), Cargo.toml (+criterion dep).

Budgets need a first calibration run to capture current numbers before the gate goes live. Expect 1-2 iterations of "CI failed because my laptop-captured baseline differs from the Linux runner" — document the calibration workflow.

description:
Reviewing recent cod commits via git log --since=6h. Pattern observed:

- 94fcdca title: 'br-frankenpandas-p6vu: add grouped resample count conformance test'. Actual diff: 183 lines including the mode_output_dtype Int64-padding fix (which was claimed by br-frankenpandas-gwa2/cc). Suggested process improvement (not code fix): cod and cc should run 'git diff --stat' before commit and detect off-bead changes; either split into two commits or cite the co-landing pair in the message. Track this as a workflow note for the swarm doctrine.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: accepted as workflow doctrine. The bead body IS the record — commits 94fcdca + 1c9467a bundled co-landed changes under off-bead titles, and the correction (run 'git diff --stat' pre-commit; split or cite co-landed beads in the message) is now captured in the bead log. **Gap:** NONE of the 21 fuzz targets use the pandas oracle as an oracle. Every target today uses either:
- Crash-only (fuzz_*_io_bytes: no panic + format round-trip)
- Invariant-only (fuzz_semantic_eq: reflexivity + symmetry + null-bridge)
- Cross-format round-trip (fuzz_format_cross_round_trip)

Per skill's Oracle Hierarchy (ranked strongest → weakest):
  1. Reference implementation  ← AVAILABLE, UNUSED
  2. Simplified shadow model
  3. Inverse operation          ← currently used
  4. Metamorphic relation
  5. Crash oracle               ← currently used

**Fix shape:** a new fuzz_differential_vs_pandas target that:
1. Reads a byte input, projects it into a tiny DataFrame (reuse fuzz_*_frame_from_bytes helpers).
2. Picks an operation from a byte tag (sum/mean/groupby-sum/merge/resample/rolling/...).
3. Calls the Rust path AND dispatches the same payload through pandas_oracle.py subprocess (or inline via PyO3 / a local oracle cache).
4. Asserts frame_a.semantic_eq(frame_b).

This catches the entire class of 'Rust produces a different answer than pandas' drift — the exact drift br-r4au / br-ch4o / br-pxv5 had to chase by hand.

Performance: oracle IPC is slow (~50ms/call), so target exec/s floor is 100 (stateful category, per skill), not 1000. Acceptable.

**Variants to consider** (file as children under this bead):
- fuzz_diff_groupby_vs_pandas (pin the multi-key emit from br-1zzp.2)
- fuzz_diff_merge_vs_pandas   (pin AG-05 alignment)
- fuzz_diff_eval_vs_pandas    (string expr engine; catches eval divergence)
- fuzz_diff_rolling_vs_pandas (pin std/var edge cases from br-fmmq)

No blocker. No current bug — just strongest-oracle-available isn't used.

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: add fuzz_semantic_eq_invariants target (lock pxv5 Null-bridging invariants)
description:
br-frankenpandas-pxv5 just added a Null(_)/Null(_) bridge to Scalar::semantic_eq. The existing fuzz corpus has no target that exercises the semantic_eq lattice, which means future regressions to that bridge (or to the Float64<->Null(NaN) bridge) won't be caught by fuzz.

Proposed target: fuzz_semantic_eq.rs:
1. Read 4 byte tags: dtype_a, dtype_b, value_a, value_b. Build Scalar a and b from each (using fuzz_feather_scalar_for_dtype-style projection).
2. Call a.semantic_eq(&b) and b.semantic_eq(&a) and assert symmetric.
3. Assert reflexive: x.semantic_eq(&x) is always true (including x = Null(NaN), Null(Null), Null(NaT), NaN-Float64).
4. Assert that any pair of Null(_) variants returns true (locks the pxv5 contract).
5. Assert that Float64(NaN).semantic_eq(Null(NaN)) is true and that Float64(NaN).semantic_eq(Null(Null)) is also true (both are missing).
6. Never panics on any byte input.

New fp-conformance pub fn fuzz_semantic_eq_bytes(input: &[u8]). Locks the equivalence-relation invariants (reflexive/symmetric/null-bridge) so the next refactor of semantic_eq can't silently break oracle parity downstream.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: implemented in fuzz_targets/fuzz_semantic_eq.rs + fp_conformance::fuzz_semantic_eq_bytes (lib.rs:5521). Locks reflexive/symmetric invariants and the Null(_)/Null(_) + Float64(NaN)<->Null(_) bridges per pxv5 contract. Landed in commit e142c21.

title: [MEDIUM] conformance: COVERAGE.md stale (814 reported vs 1249 actual fixtures; no row-MI / str accessor listings)
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Coverage Accounting Matrix rule.

**Observed state:** crates/fp-conformance/COVERAGE.md (9.8KB, last touched 2026-04-18) reports a total of 814 fixtures. Counting the actual corpus today (find crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets -maxdepth 1 -name '*.json' | wc -l) returns **1249** fixtures. The doc is **53% under-reported** — 435 fixtures landed between the 2026-04-18 snapshot and today without COVERAGE.md being regenerated.

**Also missing from COVERAGE.md:**
- The Window Functions row shows 5 fixtures; today the rolling family alone has roughly 30 (mean/sum/min/max/count/std/var across Series + DataFrame + grouped + resample).
- No row for row MultiIndex (br-frankenpandas-1zzp.* slices landed 1-5 today; slice 6 conformance coverage still open as 1zzp.5).
- No row for Excel default round-trip (br-ho6t fix + detection helper).
- No row for Series str* 50+ accessors (README says 50+ but COVERAGE.md is silent).

**Violates skill requirements:**
- Rule: "Before claiming conformance, prove it" — the table claims 214 operations / 814 fixtures but reality is closer to 430 packets / 1249 fixtures. The claim is not provable against the reported numbers.
- Rule: "Score < 0.95 for MUST clauses = NOT conformant. Ship with known gaps documented, never with unknown gaps." — unknown gaps exist because the doc doesn't list what landed.
- Compliance report generator pattern (skill's Pattern 4 template) — COVERAGE.md is hand-maintained; should be auto-generated from a traversal of fixtures/packets/ + a registry of operations under test.

**Fix shape:**
1. Add crates/fp-conformance/src/bin/coverage.rs or extend fp-ci-gates to emit COVERAGE.md from a single source of truth (scan fixtures/packets/, group by operation category, count per category, count by requirement level once that tagging lands — see sibling MEDIUM bead).
2. Wire the generator into the CI job that already runs fp-ci-gates (ci.yml 'CI gate topology' step). Fail the build if COVERAGE.md is stale.
3. Backfill the 435-fixture delta in the current doc so main is honest about coverage today.

Priority MEDIUM — doc-credibility issue, not a correctness bug. Numbers under-promise current reality, so no shipped claim is falsely elevated, but the README pulls from COVERAGE.md's narrative so the under-claim compounds upstream.

title: [MEDIUM] conformance: no RequirementLevel (MUST/SHOULD/MAY) tagging on fixtures — compliance score uncomputable
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Pattern 4 (Spec-Derived Test Matrix) + ConformanceTest trait design.

**Observed state:** every fixture under crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/ is treated as equal weight. Grep for 'RequirementLevel' or 'MUST' or 'requirement_level' in the harness code returns nothing. The fixture JSON schema has packet_id, case_id, mode (strict/hardened), operation, data payload, expected_* — no requirement_level field. run_packet_suite + run_e2e_suite report pass/fail counts but cannot distinguish "all MUST clauses pass" from "all MUSTs + SHOULDs + MAYs pass."

**Violates skill requirements:**
- Pattern 4 template explicitly carries level: RequirementLevel::{Must, Should, May}.
- Coverage Accounting Matrix header: "MUST Clauses | SHOULD Clauses | ... | Score" — currently impossible to populate.
- Rule: "Score < 0.95 for MUST clauses = NOT conformant." — we cannot even compute this score because level isn't tracked.

**Concrete impact:** if we ever need to ship with known MAY-level gaps (likely, pandas has many undocumented edge cases), the current harness can't tell the difference between a MUST-regression (ship-blocker) and a MAY-regression (acceptable divergence tracked in DISCREPANCIES.md).

**Fix shape:**
1. Add `requirement_level: Option<RequirementLevel>` to PacketFixture struct in fp-conformance/src/lib.rs (serde default = Must when absent — conservative default).
2. Enum RequirementLevel { Must, Should, May } with serde rename_all = "lowercase".
3. Backfill existing fixtures: most groupby / arithmetic / IO round-trip cases are MUST (pandas documents the contract). Display-width / error-message-text cases are MAY. String-locale behavior is SHOULD.
4. Update DifferentialReport + PacketSuiteReport to carry per-level pass/fail counts.
5. COVERAGE.md compliance matrix (see sibling br-frankenpandas-n609) groups by level. Fail CI when MUST score < 95%; WARN on SHOULD < 90%; ignore MAY regressions but log them.

Priority MEDIUM — infrastructure for future ship-decision clarity, not a correctness bug.

[HIGH] fuzz: cross-format round-trip parity fuzz target (catch IO+Equality drift)
description:
Existing fuzz harness has individual format round-trip targets (excel/parquet/feather/ipc_stream/json/csv) but each only checks 'parse → write → reparse → equals'. None of them check CROSS-FORMAT parity: parquet write of a frame should produce data that, when written through arrow IPC, contains the same logical values. Recent IO drift bugs (br-989b excel index leak, br-mxho fuzz excel leak, plus calamine 0.34.0 dependency bump) suggest format-specific assumptions can silently diverge.

Proposed target: fuzz_format_cross_round_trip.rs:
1. Synth mode: project bytes into a small DataFrame (3-8 rows, 2-3 typed columns).
2. Pick a primary format (parquet/feather/ipc_stream) from input bytes.
3. Round-trip through primary format → frame_a.
4. Round-trip through a SECONDARY format → frame_b.
5. Assert frame_a.semantic_eq(frame_b) (cross-format invariant: the lossy bits of any individual format must converge to the same logical content).
6. Assert no panic on any byte input.

New fp-conformance pub fn fuzz_format_cross_round_trip_bytes(input: &[u8]). Catches the entire class of drift where format X loses metadata that format Y preserves.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: implemented in fuzz_targets/fuzz_format_cross_round_trip.rs + fp_conformance::fuzz_format_cross_round_trip_bytes (lib.rs:5138). title: fp-types: Scalar::semantic_eq rejects cross-kind Null comparison (73 fp-conformance fixtures affected)
description:
Observed: 73 of 108 fp-conformance bootstrap-suite fixture failures share pattern 'actual=Null(NaN), expected=Null(Null)'. These are semantically-equivalent missing values flagged as mismatched because Scalar::semantic_eq falls through to PartialEq for same-variant pairs, and Null(NaN) != Null(Null) under derived PartialEq (different discriminants).

Root: crates/fp-types/src/lib.rs:95 fn semantic_eq. The Float64 <-> Null(NaN) cross-kind case is handled, but Null(Null) <-> Null(NaN) is not. When fp-frame normalizes Float64 column missing cells to Null(NaN) (dtype-specific missing marker) and the fixture oracle serializes Null(Null) (canonical missing), the semantic_eq comparison rejects them.

Fix: make semantic_eq treat any two Null variants as equal — missingness is semantically indistinguishable for oracle parity. Add (Scalar::Null(_), Scalar::Null(_)) => true before the PartialEq fallback. Preserves existing Float64<->NaN bridging.

Impact: should flip 70+ fixture cases from Fail to Pass, unblocking ci_gate_g6, packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases, differential_all_packets_green, differential_suite_produces_structured_drift, and several other top-level aggregates that cascade on the bootstrap suite.
close_reason: shipped da3a25c: semantic_eq now bridges all Null-kind pairs (Null/NaN/NaT) as semantically equal for oracle-parity checks. fp-conformance case-level: 1141/108 → 1212/37 (71 fixtures recovered in one change across abs/astype/diff/rank/round/skew/value_counts/dt_*/resample/rolling with_nulls variants). Public PartialEq unchanged. Clippy clean; fp-types 62/62 tests pass.

title: [HIGH] conformance: no aggregate counter for live-oracle ran/skipped/failed — silent-skip isn't observable
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Pattern 1 (Differential Testing) observations, combined with a fresh count.

**Observation.** grep for test function names of the form `fn live_oracle_.*_matches_pandas` in crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs returns 89 tests. These tests cover:
- DataFrameGroupBy.rolling.mean/sum/min/max/count/std/var (7 tests from br-1zzp family)
- DataFrameGroupBy.resample min/max/count/first/last/sum/mean (7 tests)
- DataFrame.apply sem_axis0/axis1 + alias variants
- Series.dt.* accessor chain
- Merge ordered (3 tests)
- Row MultiIndex construction / indexing / reshape (newly added in d35ab20 for br-1zzp.5)
- ... and ~65 more

**Gap.** `cargo test -p fp-conformance` output prints 388 passed without distinguishing how many of those 388 were FixtureExpected packets (compare against frozen JSON) vs how many were LiveLegacyPandas dispatch (compare against live pandas call). A human reviewer cannot answer "how much of our pandas parity did CI actually verify?" from the test output alone.

There's no aggregation test that asserts "at least N live_oracle_* tests ran successfully against the live oracle," only individual tests that skip silently when oracle is absent. This means:
- A CI where legacy_pandas_code is missing: 89 silent skips, test count still passes — false-green.
- A CI where a pandas upgrade breaks the oracle script: every test skips with OracleUnavailable, no one notices.
- Local-only developers: tests run; but there's no committed metric confirming "this commit's oracle run matched pandas."

**Severity.** HIGH — this is the observability half of br-frankenpandas-d6xa (CRITICAL sibling on CI-pandas-install). Even after fixing the install, we need to surface "did we actually test against pandas?" as a first-class counter.

**Fix shape:**
1. Add LiveOracleReport { ran: usize, skipped: usize, failed: usize } aggregated across all live_oracle_* tests via a lazy_static registry (each test bumps a counter on ok/skip/fail).
2. New e2e-level test that asserts report.skipped == 0 when oracle_root is present, and (once d6xa lands) fails loudly when both oracle_root is absent AND require_live_oracle=true.
3. `cargo run -p fp-conformance --bin fp-ci-gates` (the existing CI binary at ci.yml:line-CI-gate-topology) gets a new gate that emits the report to artifacts/ci/live_oracle_report.json. Governance gate (scripts/governance_gate_check.sh) reads that and fails when ran < 89.
4. Surface in README: "N live-oracle tests ran · M skipped · K failed" near the Conformance capability row.

Closes the observability loop so "we silently skipped 89 tests" becomes a loud failure instead of a silent passing build.

Depends on br-frankenpandas-d6xa — without live oracle actually running in CI, this report would just always say "ran: 0, skipped: 89" and CI would fail. They should land together.

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: add fuzz_dataframe_eval_expression target (string expr -> eval injection surface)
description:
fp-conformance dispatches DataFrameEval/Query operations through eval_str_with_locals/query_str_with_locals — string expression parsing surface that has no fuzz target. This is a high-risk parser surface (similar to CSV/JSON in attack surface) with no adversarial coverage.

Proposed target: fuzz_dataframe_eval.rs:
1. Synth mode: split bytes into (dtype_byte, expr_bytes). Build a small DataFrame with 2-4 numeric columns named a/b/c/d. Treat expr_bytes as a UTF-8 string (using lossy decode so all bytes are valid input).
2. Call eval_str_with_locals(&expr_str, &frame, &locals, &policy, &mut ledger).
3. Assert: never panics for any input. Errors are FrameError::CompatibilityRejected or ExprError variants — not unwraps or arithmetic overflow panics.
4. For successful evals, assert output Series length == frame index length.

New fp-conformance pub fn fuzz_dataframe_eval_bytes(input: &[u8]). Catches parser regressions, expression-injection vectors, and arithmetic-overflow panics in the eval path — none of which are currently covered.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: implemented in fuzz_targets/fuzz_dataframe_eval.rs + fp_conformance::fuzz_dataframe_eval_bytes (lib.rs:5431). Exercises the string-expr eval injection surface with RuntimePolicy::strict()/hardened() switching and EvidenceLedger observation; asserts no panic + result length parity with input frame. Harness name is fuzz_dataframe_eval rather than the proposed fuzz_dataframe_eval_expression (same spec). Landed in commit 240a620.

title: [MEDIUM] fp-conformance bootstrap_cases needs differential evidence ledger emission for the 37 remaining fixture failures
description:
Reviewing packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases: with cc's pxv5 semantic_eq fix landed, fixture-level results moved from 1141/108 → 1212/37. The 37 remaining failures span at least 18 distinct case_ids (dataframe_resample_*, series_dt_ceil/date_*, dataframe_pct_change/skew/round/value_counts/diff/abs/astype/memory_usage with_nulls variants, str_repeat_n / str_sub gaps).

Currently the harness logs only 'evidence_records: 0' for each Pass case and {report:?} as a single Debug dump on failure. There is no on-disk evidence ledger that captures per-fixture diffs in a machine-readable form — so each agent picking up a fixture fix has to re-run the harness, parse the {report:?} blob, and rediscover which case to fix.

Suggested follow-up:
1. In every Fail CaseResult, emit an evidence record (already counted as 'evidence_records' field) containing {expected_dtype, actual_dtype, first_mismatch_idx, first_mismatch_actual, first_mismatch_expected, ledger_id} via fp_runtime::EvidenceLedger.
2. Persist the ledger as JSONL artifacts under artifacts/conformance/<packet_id>/<case_id>.jsonl so subsequent reviewers can  for known-failing-pattern signatures (e.g. 'all dataframe_resample_*_with_nulls share the same root cause').
3. Add an --emit-evidence flag to fp-conformance-cli so CI can produce evidence in a single pass without rerunning per-test.

This is testing-harness hardening, not parity work; pairs naturally with 44vd (cascade-surface).
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: per-case failure evidence ledger emission from bootstrap_cases landed in commit b2ad0cb (test(conformance): emit per-case failure evidence ledgers). fp-conformance lib test suite is 373/0 green, no remaining fixture failures to instrument.

title: [HIGH] fuzz: no CI regression workflow or persistent corpus — fuzz fixes can silently regress
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill audit to fuzz/ dir.

**Observed state:**
- 21 fuzz targets exist under fuzz/fuzz_targets/
- fuzz/Cargo.toml carries [[bin]] blocks for each
- .github/workflows/ci.yml has format, check, clippy, test, conformance, bench, phase2c, governance — NO fuzz step
- fuzz/corpus/ does not exist
- fuzz/artifacts/ does not exist

**Violates skill rules:**
- Rule 10 (Every crash artifact becomes a regression test or it WILL regress) — no regression corpus
- Rule 14 (Fuzzing ROI follows a power law; invest in breadth) — harnesses exist but never re-run automatically
- Checklist "CI runs regression corpus on every PR" — not satisfied
- Checklist "Nightly continuous fuzzing scheduled" — not satisfied

**Concrete risk:** a crash found on day N and fixed on day N+1 is NOT locked in. Nothing on main exercises the fuzz targets after the fix. Any future regression that reintroduces the bug passes `cargo test` silently.

**Fix shape:**
1. Add fuzz/corpus/<target>/ with at least the empty seed + 3 boundary seeds per target (21 targets × 4 seeds = 84 files, small commit).
2. Add fuzz/artifacts/<target>/ (gitignored except for README.md listing known regression inputs once they arrive).
3. New CI job `fuzz-regression` that runs `cargo +nightly fuzz run <target> -- -runs=10000` for each target against the committed corpus. Exit non-zero on any new crash.
4. Optional: schedule a nightly GH Actions run that fuzzes each target for ~60s with the accumulated corpus, uploads coverage, opens a bead on new crashes (closes the loop).
5. Document the regression workflow in fuzz/README.md: found-a-crash → minimize (cargo fuzz tmin) → add to corpus → commit → CI locks it in.

No existing crashes known (test suite is 3161/0/13) — this is a pure infrastructure bead, not a bug bead. Priority HIGH because the risk is silent regression of any fuzz fix we've ever landed.
